Test: Haemoglobin (male)
Normal Range: 13-17 g/dL
Critical Value: < 7 g/dL
Action: Activate transfusion protocol

Test: Haemoglobin (female)
Normal Range: 12-15 g/dL
Critical Value: < 7 g/dL
Action: Activate transfusion protocol

Test: WBC count
Normal Range: 4-11 × 10⁹/L
Critical Value: > 30 × 10⁹/L
Action: Haematology consult

Test: Platelet count
Normal Range: 150-400 × 10⁹/L
Critical Value: < 50 × 10⁹/L
Action: Bleeding-risk protocol

Test: PT / INR
Normal Range: INR 0.9-1.2
Critical Value: INR > 5
Action: Withhold anticoagulant; review

Test: aPTT
Normal Range: 26-36 s
Critical Value: > 90 s
Action: Review heparin; bleeding risk

Test: D-dimer
Normal Range: < 0.5 µg/mL
Critical Value: Marked rise
Action: Consider VTE; correlate clinically

Test: Ferritin
Normal Range: 30-300 ng/mL
Critical Value: -
Action: Low: iron deficiency; high: inflammation

Test: ESR
Normal Range: M<15, F < 20 mm/hr
Critical Value: -
Action: Non-specific inflammation marker

Test: Sodium
Normal Range: 135-145 mEq/L
Critical / Significant: < 120 or > 160
Note: Correct slowly

Test: Potassium
Normal Range: 3.5-5.0 mEq/L
Critical / Significant: < 3.0 or > 6.0
Note: Cardiac monitoring if extreme

Test: Creatinine
Normal Range: 0.6-1.3 mg/dL
Critical / Significant: Rapid rise
Note: Assess AKI; review nephrotoxics

Test: Urea
Normal Range: 15-45 mg/dL
Critical / Significant: -
Note: Correlate with creatinine

Test: Glucose (fasting)
Normal Range: 70-100 mg/dL
Critical / Significant: < 50 or > 400
Note: Treat hypo-/hyperglycaemia

Test: HbA1c
Normal Range: < 5.7%
Critical / Significant: ≥ 6.5% = diabetes
Note: Glycaemic control over ~3 months

Test: ALT / AST
Normal Range: < 40 U/L
Critical / Significant: > 10× ULN
Note: Hepatocellular injury

Test: ALP
Normal Range: 40-130 U/L
Critical / Significant: -
Note: Cholestasis / bone

Test: Bilirubin (total)
Normal Range: 0.3-1.2 mg/dL
Critical / Significant: -
Note: Jaundice if elevated

Test: Troponin I
Normal Range: < 0.04 ng/mL
Critical / Significant: > 0.4 = significant
Note: Serial; correlate with ECG

Test: CRP
Normal Range: < 5 mg/L
Critical / Significant: Marked rise
Note: Inflammation / infection

Test: Procalcitonin
Normal Range: < 0.5 ng/mL
Critical / Significant: > 2 ng/mL
Note: Suggests bacterial sepsis

Dipstick Finding: Protein +
Interpretation: Possible renal disease / UTI
Next Step: Quantify (ACR); repeat

Dipstick Finding: Glucose +
Interpretation: Hyperglycaemia / low renal threshold
Next Step: Check blood glucose

Dipstick Finding: Blood +
Interpretation: Haematuria -infection, stones, malignancy
Next Step: Microscopy; imaging if persistent

Dipstick Finding: Nitrites +
Interpretation: Gram-negative bacteriuria
Next Step: Send culture; treat if symptomatic

Dipstick Finding: Leucocytes +
Interpretation: Pyuria / inflammation
Next Step: Correlate with nitrites & symptoms

Dipstick Finding: Nitrites + & Leucocytes +
Interpretation: Strongly suggests UTI
Next Step: Treat per antibiogram

Parameter: Rate
Normal: 60-100 bpm
Abnormal Significance: < 60 brady; > 100 tachy

Parameter: Rhythm
Normal: Sinus
Abnormal Significance: AF, flutter, VT, SVT -classify

Parameter: P-R interval
Normal: 120-200 ms
Abnormal Significance: > 200 ms = 1° AV block

Parameter: QRS duration
Normal: < 120 ms
Abnormal Significance: > 120 ms = bundle branch block

Parameter: QTc
Normal: < 440 ms (M), < 460 ms (F)
Abnormal Significance: Prolonged →arrhythmia risk

Parameter: ST elevation
Normal: Isoelectric
Abnormal Significance: > 1 mmin ≥ 2 contiguous leads = STEMI protocol

Parameter: pH
Normal Range: 7.35-7.45
Interpretation: < 7.35 acidosis; > 7.45 alkalosis

Parameter: PaCO₂
Normal Range: 35-45 mmHg
Interpretation: Respiratory component

Parameter: HCO₃⁻
Normal Range: 22-26 mEq/L
Interpretation: Metabolic component

Parameter: PaO₂
Normal Range: 80-100 mmHg
Interpretation: < 60 = respiratory failure

Parameter: Lactate
Normal Range: 0.5-2.2 mmol/L
Interpretation: > 4 suggests hypoperfusion / sepsis

Test: TSH
Normal Range: 0.4-4.0 mIU/L
Note: First-line thyroid screen

Test: Free T4
Normal Range: 0.8-1.8 ng/dL
Note: Confirms thyroid status

Test: BNP / NT-proBNP
Normal Range: Age-dependent
Note: Elevated in heart failure

Test: CK-MB
Normal Range: < 5 ng/mL
Note: Cardiac injury (older marker)

Test: Lactate (sepsis)
Normal Range: < 2 mmol/L
Note: ≥ 2 triggers sepsis-6 review

Modality: Chest X-ray (CXR)
Urgent means: Pneumothorax, free air, mis-placed line
Routine TAT: Reported within 24 h

Modality: CT head
Urgent means: Bleed, mass effect, acute infarct
Routine TAT: Reported within 12 h

Modality: USS abdomen
Urgent means: Free fluid, obstruction, aneurysm
Routine TAT: Reported within 24 h

Specimen / Test: Blood culture
Preliminary: Gram stain at flagging (~12-24 h)
Final Report: 48-72 h (5 days if negative)

Specimen / Test: Urine culture
Preliminary: -
Final Report: 24-48 h

Specimen / Test: Sputum culture
Preliminary: Gram stain same day
Final Report: 48-72 h

Specimen / Test: CSF analysis
Preliminary: Urgent Gram stain < 1 h
Final Report: Culture 48-72 h

Specimen / Test: Stool culture
Preliminary: -
Final Report: 48-72 h

Marker: PSA

Marker: CA-125

Marker: CEA

Marker: AFP
Associated With: Hepatocellular / germ cell
Note: Correlate with imaging

Line: First-line
Drug &Dose: Metformin 500 mg BD with meals
Notes: Titrate to 1000 mg BD over 4 weeks

Line: Second-line
Drug &Dose: Add Glipizide 5 mg OD
Notes: If HbA1c > 7.5% after 3 months on Metformin

Line: Third-line
Drug &Dose: Consider SGLT2 inhibitor / GLP-1 agonist
Notes: Specialist-guided; review renal status

Line: First-line
Drug &Dose: Amlodipine 5 mg OD
Cautions: Titrate to 10 mg if target not met in 4 weeks; ankle oedema

Line: Second-line
Drug &Dose: Add Telmisartan 40 mg OD
Cautions: Avoid in pregnancy; monitor K⁺ and creatinine

Line: Add-on
Drug &Dose: Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5-25 mg OD
Cautions: Monitor electrolytes; caution in gout

Section: Equipment Operation & Maintenance Manual

Combined Reference for Biomedical Technicians

MediAssist Health Network

Biomedical Engineering Department MediAssist Health Network Document ref: BME-EQManual-012 · Version 7.0

Access: Technicians & Admin This  combined  operation  and  maintenance  manual  covers  the  four  most  common  equipment  categories  deployed across  MediAssist  hospitals.  It  is  intended  for  biomedical  technicians  and  trained  clinical  users.  Always  observe  the device's own labelling and the manufacturer's instructions for use where they are more specific than this manual.

Section: Conventions

Asset tags follow the format EQ-CAMPUS-XXXX (e.g. EQ-HYD-1042). Fault codes logged here map directly to the fault\_code field in the maintenance ticketing system.

Section: A. Patient Monitoring System - MediAssist BM-500

Multi-parameter  monitor  for  ECG,  SpO₂,  NIBP,  respiratory  rate  and  temperature.  12-inch  colour  display;  internal battery backup of 4 hours. Serial-number format: BM500-YYYY-NNNNN .

Section: Setup procedure

- 1 Power on and allow the self-test to complete (~45 seconds).
- 2 Enter the patient ID.
- 3 Connect leads and probes in order: ECG leads first, then the SpO₂ probe, then the BP cuff.
- 4 Confirm a valid trace on each parameter before leaving the bedside.

Section: Alarm parameter defaults and adjustable ranges

| Parameter     | Default Low   | Default High   | Adjustable Range   |
|---------------|---------------|----------------|--------------------|
| SpO₂          | 90%           | 100%           | 80-100%            |
| Heart Rate    | 50 bpm        | 120 bpm        | 30-250 bpm         |
| NIBP Systolic | 90 mmHg       | 160 mmHg       | 60-240 mmHg        |
| Temperature   | 35.5 °C       | 38.5 °C        | 34-42 °C           |

Section: Fault codes

| Code   | Meaning                 | Action                                         |
|--------|-------------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| E-01   | ECG lead off            | Re-attach / replace lead                       |
| E-02   | SpO₂ probe disconnected | Reconnect or replace probe                     |
| E-03   | NIBP cuff leak          | Check cuff and tubing; replace cuff            |
| E-07   | Battery low (< 15%)     | Connect to mains; service battery if recurrent |
| E-12   | Internal sensor failure | Remove from service immediately; log in CMMS   |

Section: Maintenance

- Daily: clean display with a 70% IPA wipe; verify alarm audibility; check battery charge.
- Monthly: SpO₂ verification against a reference pulse oximeter.
- 6-monthly: NIBP calibration by the biomedical team (tolerance ±3 mmHg).

Section: Remove from service

Fault E-12 indicates an internal sensor failure - the unit must be removed from service immediately and a maintenance ticket raised.

Section: B. Infusion Pump - DriveFlow IP-200

Syringe  and  volumetric  infusion  pump  with  Dose  Rate  Error  Reduction  (DERR)  software  and  a  drug  library  of  150 pre-programmed protocols. Serial-number format: IP200-YYYY-NNNNN .

Section: Programming steps

- 1 Select the infusion mode (syringe or volumetric).
- 2 Enter the drug name from the on-board library.
- 3 Enter the patient weight; the system calculates the safe dose range.
- 4 Confirm the rate against the displayed limits.
- 5 Start the infusion and verify the running display.

Section: Occlusion pressure alarm settings

- Default - 300 mmHg.
- Venous lines - reduce the threshold to 200 mmHg.
- Arterial lines - maintain the threshold at 300 mmHg.

Section: High-alert drug protocols (drug library)

The following carry hard limits (cannot be overridden) and soft limits (require confirmation): Dopamine, Dobutamine, Noradrenaline, Insulin, Heparin, Morphine.

Section: Fault codes

| Code   | Meaning                      | Action                                           |
|--------|------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| F-01   | Air in line                  | Purge line; check connections                    |
| F-03   | Occlusion                    | Check for kinks/clamps; restart                  |
| F-05   | Door open                    | Close door securely                              |
| F-08   | Battery failure              | Service battery; use mains                       |
| F-12   | Drug library update required | Remove from service; update library; log in CMMS |

Section: Maintenance schedule

- After each patient use: clean with 70% IPA.
- Weekly: functional check by the biomedical team.
- Annual: full service by an authorised service engineer.
- Every 18 months: replace the battery.

Section: C. Autoclave Steriliser - SterilPro 3000

134-litre  chamber  steam  steriliser  supporting  gravity  and  pre-vacuum  cycles,  validated  for  wrapped  instruments, unwrapped instruments and porous loads. Serial-number format: SP3000-YYYY-NNNNN .

Section: Cycle selection

| Cycle          | Temperature   | Pressure   | Hold Time   | Suitable For                        |
|----------------|---------------|------------|-------------|-------------------------------------|
| Gravity 121    | 121 °C        | 103 kPa    | 15 min      | Unwrapped instruments, fluids       |
| Gravity 134    | 134 °C        | 206 kPa    | 3 min       | Unwrapped solid instruments         |
| Pre-vacuum 134 | 134 °C        | 206 kPa    | 3.5 min     | Wrapped sets, porous & hollow loads |

Section: Routine testing

- Bowie-Dick test: mandatory every morning before the first load. A fail means do not use - call the biomedical team.
- Biological indicator (BI) test: weekly. A positive result means quarantine all loads from that week and notify infection control.

Section: Daily log requirements

Record for every cycle: cycle number, cycle type, temperature/pressure chart, pass/fail result, operator ID, and load contents.

Section: Fault codes

| Code   | Meaning                   | Action                                    |
|--------|---------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| E-01   | Door seal failure         | Inspect/replace gasket; leak test         |
| E-04   | Steam generator low water | Refill / check supply                     |
| E-07   | Temperature sensor fault  | Do not use; call biomedical               |
| E-11   | Vacuum pump failure       | Do not run pre-vacuum cycle; service pump |

Section: D. Portable X-Ray Unit - RadiPro MX-150

150 kW mobile X-ray unit with a wireless DR panel, DICOM compatibility and battery operation for ~50 exposures per charge. Serial-number format: MX150-YYYY-NNNNN .

Section: Technique chart

| Body Part             |   kVp |   mAs | Grid   | Notes               |
|-----------------------|-------|-------|--------|---------------------|
| Chest AP (adult)      |    90 |     5 | No     | Erect preferred     |
| Chest AP (paediatric) |    70 |     3 | No     | Short exposure time |
| Abdomen AP            |    80 |    15 | Yes    | Expiration phase    |
| Pelvis                |    85 |    20 | Yes    | Gonad shielding     |
| Knee AP               |    65 |     8 | No     | -                   |

Section: Radiation safety

- All personnel stand > 2 m from the unit, or behind a protective barrier, during exposure.
- The operator must wear a dosimetry badge at all times.
- Log the exposure count weekly.

Section: Fault codes

| Code   | Meaning               | Action                           |
|--------|-----------------------|----------------------------------|
| F-02   | DR panel not detected | Re-pair panel; check battery     |
| F-05   | Battery below 20%     | Charge before use                |
| F-09   | kV generator fault    | Remove from service; log in CMMS |

Section: Battery & preventive maintenance

- Charge after each session; do not store fully discharged; replace every 3 years.
- Monthly: clean, battery check, exposure-counter log.
- Quarterly: HV cable inspection, collimator check.
- Annual: kV/mAs calibration by a service engineer, plus a radiation-leakage test.

Section: Remove from service

Fault F-09 (kV generator fault) requires the unit to be removed from service and logged in the CMMS. Do not attempt further exposures.

Section: E. Maintenance & Fault-Code Summary

Quick  cross-reference  of  the  fault  codes  that  mandate  immediate  removal  from  service.  These  are  the  codes  that should appear on escalated maintenance tickets.

| Code   | Device           | Meaning                      | Mandatory Action                  |
|--------|------------------|------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| E-12   | BM-500 Monitor   | Internal sensor failure      | Remove from service; raise ticket |
| F-12   | DriveFlow IP-200 | Drug library update required | Remove from service; raise ticket |
| F-09   | RadiPro MX-150   | kV generator fault           | Remove from service; raise ticket |

Section: F. Preventive Maintenance Calendar (summary)

| Equipment        | Daily          | Weekly           | Monthly                    | Annual                           |
|------------------|----------------|------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------------|
| BM-500 Monitor   | Clean, battery | -                | SpO₂ check; NIBP 6-monthly | Full service                     |
| DriveFlow IP-200 | Clean per use  | Functional check | -                          | Service + 18-mo battery          |
| SterilPro 3000   | Bowie-Dick     | BI test          | Seal inspection            | Validation & calibration         |
| RadiPro MX-150   | -              | Exposure log     | Clean, battery             | kV/mAs calibration; leakage test |

Section: Record-keeping

All preventive maintenance and faults must be recorded in the CMMS / maintenance ticketing system. Escalated tickets are reviewed weekly by the Biomedical Engineering lead.

Section: G. Commissioning & Acceptance Testing

Every new or repaired device must pass commissioning before clinical use. The biomedical team records the result against the asset tag in the CMMS, checking that:

- Electrical safety - earth leakage is within IEC 60601 limits.
- Functional test - all parameters read within manufacturer tolerance.
- Calibration certificate - is valid and on file.
- Asset tagging - the EQ-CAMPUS-XXXX label is applied and registered.
- User training - ward staff are trained and the sign-off is recorded.

Section: Mandatory

No device may be handed to biomedical engineering or an external service engineer until it has been decontaminated and a decontamination certificate attached. This protects service staff from exposure.

- Surface-clean per the device section above (70% IPA unless contraindicated).
- Visibly soiled equipment must be cleaned and, where applicable, disinfected before transport.
- Attach the completed decontamination certificate to the device and the ticket.

Section: I. Common Operator Errors & Troubleshooting

| Symptom                 | Likely Cause                    | First Check                            |
|-------------------------|---------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| No SpO₂ reading         | Probe placement / perfusion     | Reposition probe; warm the digit       |
| NIBP repeatedly retries | Cuff size / patient movement    | Use correct cuff size; keep limb still |
| Infusion not starting   | Door not latched / clamp closed | Close door; open the roller clamp      |
| Autoclave cycle aborts  | Overloaded chamber / wet load   | Reduce load; ensure correct packing    |
| X-ray panel image blank | Panel not paired / low battery  | Re-pair DR panel; charge battery       |

Section: Code of Conduct & Ethics Policy

Behavioural Standards, Conflict of Interest and Patient Privacy

MediAssist Health Network Office of Compliance & Human Resources Document ref: COMP-COC-003 · Version 5.2 Applies to: All staff and contractors

Section: 1. Professional Conduct

Every MediAssist employee is expected to act with professionalism at all times. This includes punctuality, respectful communication, and respect for the clinical and administrative hierarchy.

Section: Zero tolerance

MediAssist maintains zero tolerance for verbal or physical abuse directed at patients, visitors or colleagues. Substantiated abuse is treated as gross misconduct.

Section: 2. Patient Confidentiality

- Patient records, diagnoses and treatment details must never be disclosed outside authorised clinical and billing channels.
- Staff are bound by HIPAA-equivalent obligations under the Indian IT Act and applicable data-protection rules.
- Photographing patients , their records or their charts on personal devices is strictly prohibited.

Section: 3. Conflict of Interest

- Staff must not refer patients to external facilities, laboratories or pharmacies in which they hold a financial interest.
- Doctors must disclose any relationship with pharmaceutical companies (speaking fees, sponsorships, advisory roles) to the Office of Compliance annually.

Section: 4. Social Media Policy

- No posting of patient images, case details or identifiable information on any platform.
- No disparagement of MediAssist, colleagues, or competitor hospitals online.
- Personal opinions must not be presented as the official position of MediAssist.

Section: 5. Gifts & Gratuity

Gifts from patients or vendors are governed by value thresholds:

- Gifts valued up to ₹500 are permitted; use professional judgement.
- Gifts valued above ₹500 must be declared to administration.
- Gifts valued above ₹2,000 must be refused, or deposited with administration.

Section: 6. Substance Abuse

- Zero tolerance for reporting to duty under the influence of alcohol or drugs.
- Clinical staff involved in an adverse patient event may be required to undergo mandatory testing as part of the investigation.

Section: 7. Anti-Harassment Policy (POSH)

- MediAssist has a constituted POSH (Prevention of Sexual Harassment) Committee at each campus, chaired by a senior woman officer with an external member.
- Complaints may be filed in writing or by email to posh@mediassist.in.
- Investigations are completed within a 15-day internal timeline (statutory limit 90 days); penalties range up to termination.

Section: 8. Data & Systems

- No installation of unauthorised software on hospital devices.
- No sharing of login credentials under any circumstances.
- Suspected data breaches must be reported to IT Security within 2 hours of discovery.

Section: 9. Disciplinary Process

Disciplinary action follows a graduated process, proportionate to the conduct, escalating through four stages:

- 1 Verbal warning (documented).
- 2 Written warning.
- 3 Final written warning.
- 4 Termination.

Section: Gross misconduct

Gross misconduct - including patient harm, data theft, billing fraud, or assault - may result in immediate termination without progressing through the stages above, subject to due process.

Section: 10. Anti-Bribery & Anti-Corruption

- No employee may offer, solicit or accept any bribe, kickback or improper inducement in connection with referrals, procurement, billing or recruitment.
- Facilitation payments to expedite routine approvals are prohibited.
- Commissions for patient referrals ("cut practice") are strictly forbidden and may attract legal action in addition to termination.

Section: 11. Vendor & Procurement Conduct

- All vendor selection must follow the documented procurement process with at least three quotations for purchases above ₹50,000.
- Staff with a personal or family interest in a vendor must recuse themselves and declare the interest to the Office of Compliance.

Section: 12. Whistleblower Protection

MediAssist encourages staff to report genuine concerns about unethical or illegal conduct without fear of retaliation.

- Concerns may be raised confidentially to ethics@mediassist.in or through the anonymous ethics hotline.
- Good-faith reporters are protected; retaliation against a whistleblower is itself a disciplinary offence.
- Knowingly false or malicious reports, however, are treated as misconduct.

Section: Declaration

Every employee signs an annual declaration confirming they have read, understood and will abide by this Code of Conduct. The signed declaration is retained in your personnel file on the HR Portal.

Section: General Staff FAQs

Quick Answers to the Questions Our Employees Ask Most Often

MediAssist Health Network Human Resources Department Document ref: HR-FAQ-004 · Version 3.0 Applies to: All staff This  FAQ  collects  the  questions  our  employees  ask  most  often,  grouped  by  theme.  For  detailed  rules,  refer  to  the relevant policy document. Questions are numbered continuously for easy reference.

Section: Q1. When is salary credited each month?

Salaries are credited on the last working day of every month directly to your registered bank account via NEFT.

Section: Q2. How do I access my payslips?

Log in to the HR Portal and navigate to Payroll > My Payslips . Payslips for the last 36 months are available for download as PDF.

Section: Q3. What is the Provident Fund (PF) contribution rate?

12% employee and 12% employer of basic salary, as per EPFO norms. Your UAN is printed on your payslip.

Section: Q4. How do I update my bank account details for salary?

Raise a request under HR Portal > Profile > Bank Details and upload a cancelled cheque. Changes are verified by HR and take effect from the next payroll cycle.

Section: Q5. What health insurance is provided to staff?

All permanent employees are covered by a family floater of ₹5,00,000 per year under a group policy with New India Assurance, covering self, spouse and two children.

Section: Q6. Is there a gratuity benefit?

Yes. Gratuity is payable as per the Payment of Gratuity Act after 5 years of continuous service, calculated on your last drawn basic salary.

Section: Q7. How do I reset my password for MediAssist systems?

Use  the self-service  reset link  on  the  login  page,  or  call  IT  Support  at  ext.  2200.  Passwords  must  be  at  least  12 characters and are rotated every 90 days.

Section: Q8. What should I do if my laptop is lost or stolen?

Report it immediately to IT Security (ithelp@mediassist.in) and your supervisor. The device will be remotely locked and wiped; file a police complaint for company-issued hardware.

Section: Q9. How do I request access to a new system or module?

Raise  a  request  on  the  IT  Service  Desk  portal  with  HOD  approval.  Access  follows  least-privilege  and  is  typically provisioned within two working days.

Section: Q10. Is personal use of hospital Wi-Fi permitted?

Limited personal use of the guest Wi-Fi network is permitted. The clinical network is strictly for work devices and must not be used for personal browsing.

Section: Q11. Who do I contact for a clinical software issue during a shift?

Call the 24×7 IT Support hotline at ext. 2200; clinical-system incidents are prioritised as P1 and responded to within 15 minutes.

Section: Q12. Where is the staff cafeteria and what are the timings?

The staff cafeteria is on the lower ground floor of each campus, open 7:00 am - 11:00 pm , with subsidised meals for all staff grades.

Section: Q13. Is there a crèche facility for staff with children?

Yes. A crèche operates at the Hyderabad Central and Pune Speciality campuses for children aged 6 months to 6 years, available to staff on all shifts.

Section: Q14. How do I book a conference room?

Conference rooms are booked through Facilities > Room Booking on the intranet, up to 30 days in advance.

Section: Q15. Where is parking allocated for each grade of staff?

Basement levels  B1-B2  are  allocated  by  grade  and  displayed  on  your  access  card  zone.  Two-wheeler  parking  is  at ground level; EV charging bays are on B1.

Section: Q16. How do I apply for a leave of absence?

All leave is applied for on the HR Portal .  Notice periods and approval chains are detailed in the Leave & Attendance Policy.

Section: Q17. Who approves leave for doctors in the ICU department?

Leave for ICU doctors is approved by the ICU department head (HOD) ;  any  leave longer than five days additionally requires the Medical Director's noting to ensure roster cover.

Section: Q18. How do I get an experience letter or employment certificate?

Request it under HR Portal > Letters ; standard letters are issued within five working days.

Section: Q19. What is the notice period for resignation?

30 days for non-clinical staff and 60 days for clinical staff , as specified in your appointment letter.

Section: Q20. Can I transfer to another campus?

Internal transfers are considered against vacancies. Apply via the Internal Job Posting board on the HR Portal with HOD endorsement.

Section: Q21. How is my annual appraisal conducted?

Appraisals run each April on a goal-based framework, with a mid-year check-in in October. Ratings are discussed with your reporting manager before finalisation.

Section: Q22. What is the fire evacuation procedure?

On  hearing  the  fire  alarm,  stop  work,  help  any  patients  nearby,  and  proceed  calmly  to  the  nearest fire  exit and assembly point. Do not use lifts. Floor wardens (yellow vests) direct the evacuation.

Section: Q23. Who do I call in a medical emergency involving a staff member?

Dial 7777 from any internal extension to activate the Code Blue medical emergency team.

Section: Q24. What should I do if I witness a patient fall?

Do not move the patient; call for clinical help immediately, ensure the area is safe, and the attending nurse will assess and document the incident per protocol.

Section: Q25. How do I report a workplace safety hazard?

Report hazards via Facilities > Safety Report or to your floor warden. Urgent hazards (spills, exposed wiring) should be reported by phone at once.

Section: Leave & Attendance Policy

Entitlements, Application Procedures and Attendance Rules

MediAssist Health Network Human Resources Policy Manual Document ref: HR-LEAVE-002 · Version 4.1 Applies to: All permanent employees

Section: 1. Scope

This policy applies to all permanent employees of MediAssist Health Network across every campus and clinic. Contract and locum staff are governed by separate terms specified in their individual engagement letters. Where a statutory entitlement exceeds the provisions below, the statutory entitlement applies.

Section: 2. Leave Types and Entitlements

Leave  entitlements  differ  between  clinical  staff  (doctors  and  nurses)  and  non-clinical  staff,  reflecting  roster  and continuity-of-care requirements.

| Leave Type        | Clinical Staff   | Non-Clinical   | Notes                                         |
|-------------------|------------------|----------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| Earned Leave (EL) | 15 days/yr       | 18 days/yr     | Carry forward max 30 days                     |
| Sick Leave (SL)   | 12 days/yr       | 12 days/yr     | Medical certificate beyond 3 consecutive days |
| Casual Leave (CL) | 8 days/yr        | 10 days/yr     | Cannot be clubbed with EL                     |
| Maternity Leave   | 182 days         | 182 days       | Per Maternity Benefit Act, 1961               |
| Paternity Leave   | 7 days           | 7 days         | Within 3 months of child's birth              |
| Bereavement Leave | 3 days           | 3 days         | Immediate family only                         |
| Study Leave       | 5 days/yr        | Not applicable | CME / certification courses only              |
| Compensatory Off  | As accrued       | As accrued     | Avail within 60 days of accrual               |

Section: 3. Leave Application Process

All leave is applied for and approved through the MediAssist HR Portal . Verbal approvals are not valid. Minimum notice periods and approval routes are:

- Earned Leave (EL) - apply at least 7 days in advance; supervisor approves, escalating to the HOD if longer than 5 days.
- Casual Leave (CL) - apply at least 2 days in advance; immediate supervisor approves.
- Sick Leave (SL) - notify the same day via the app; the supervisor regularises it on your return.

Section: Approval rule

Any leave longer than five consecutive days requires department head (HOD) approval, regardless of leave type.

Section: 4. Attendance Tracking

- Attendance is captured by biometric punch and the mobile check-in app (geo-fenced to campus).
- A grace period of 10 minutes applies to shift start times.
- Late arrival beyond the grace period is recorded; after 3 such occurrences in a calendar month , each further late arrival is marked as a half-day.
- Missed punches must be regularised on the HR Portal within 48 hours with supervisor approval.

Section: 5. On-Call & Duty Roster

Doctors and nurses participate in a rotational on-call roster published by the unit in-charge two weeks in advance. On-call compensation is paid per night:

- Resident doctors - ₹800 per night, doubling to ₹1,600 on Sundays and public holidays.
- Nurses - ₹500 per night, doubling to ₹1,000 on Sundays and public holidays.

Section: 6. Public Holidays

- 10 fixed national holidays plus 3 floating regional holidays per calendar year, published each January.
- Clinical staff required to work on a public holiday receive compensatory off plus 1.5× pay for that day.

Section: 7. Leave Encashment

- Accumulated Earned Leave above the 30-day carry-forward cap is encashed annually each March, calculated on basic salary.
- On separation, the full Earned Leave balance is encashed in the final settlement.
- Sick, Casual and Compensatory leave are not encashable.

Section: 8. Leave Without Pay (LOP) & Special Cases

- Leave Without Pay (LOP) is granted only when paid leave is exhausted and requires HOD plus HR approval; it does not count towards service for increments.
- Half-day leave may be availed against CL in 0.5-day units via the HR Portal.
- Sandwich rule: intervening weekly-offs and holidays between two LOP days are counted as LOP.
- Leave during probation: only Sick and Casual leave may be availed; Earned Leave accrues but is availed only after confirmation.

Section: 9. Public Holiday List (illustrative)

Ten  fixed  national  holidays  are  observed  each  year,  with  regional  floating  holidays  added  per  campus  state.  The illustrative list below is finalised and published every January:

- 1 Republic Day (26 January)
- 2 Holi
- 3 Good Friday
- 4 May Day (1 May)
- 5 Independence Day (15 August)
- 6 Ganesh Chaturthi (regional / floating)
- 7 Gandhi Jayanti (2 October)
- 8 Dussehra
- 9 Deepavali
- 10 Christmas (25 December)

Section: Important

Absence from duty without information for 10 or more consecutive days is treated as voluntary abandonment of service and may lead to termination after due process. If you face an emergency preventing communication, inform HR at the earliest opportunity.

Section: MediAssist Staff Handbook

A Welcome Guide for Every MediAssist Employee

MediAssist Health Network Human Resources Department Document ref: HR-HBK-001 · Version 6.0 Applies to: All staff, all campuses

Section: 1. About MediAssist Health Network

MediAssist Health Network was founded in 2008 in Hyderabad with a single 90-bed hospital and a simple conviction: that  high-quality,  ethical  healthcare  should  be  accessible  to  the  communities  it  serves.  Over  the  years  that  single hospital has grown into one of South and West India's most respected mid-sized private hospital groups.

Today the Network operates 12 hospitals across Telangana, Karnataka and Maharashtra, supported by more than 40 clinics and diagnostic centres. We are a family of approximately 8,500 staff - doctors, nurses, technicians, billing and administrative colleagues - caring for over two million patient visits every year. Our registered head office remains in Hyderabad, Telangana.

Section: Welcome

This handbook is your primary onboarding reference. Keep it accessible during your first 90 days. Where this handbook and a specific policy document differ, the specific policy document prevails.

Section: 2. Organisational Structure

MediAssist follows a clear governance and reporting hierarchy. Understanding where you sit in this structure helps you know who to approach for approvals, escalations and support.

The reporting hierarchy runs through six levels:

- Board of Directors - governance, strategy and statutory oversight.
- Chief Executive Officer (CEO) - network-wide operations and performance.
- CMO / CTO / CFO - clinical, technology and finance leadership respectively.
- Department Heads (HODs) - management of clinical and non-clinical departments.
- Senior Staff - consultants, senior nurses and unit in-charges.
- Junior Staff - resident doctors, staff nurses, technicians and executives.

Section: Patient First

Every decision is measured against one question: is this good for the patient? Clinical and non-clinical staff alike are expected to place patient safety, dignity and comfort ahead of convenience.

Section: Integrity

We are honest in our clinical records, our billing, and our dealings with patients, insurers and each other. There is no acceptable shortcut around the truth.

Section: Clinical Excellence

We practise evidence-based medicine, follow our Standard Treatment Protocols, and continually audit our outcomes against national benchmarks.

Section: Continuous Learning

Medicine and technology never stand still. We invest in CME, certifications and structured on-the-job training for every grade of staff.

Staff

Section: Team Respect

A hospital runs on teamwork. We treat colleagues across every department and grade with courtesy, and we have zero tolerance for harassment or abuse.

Section: 4. Facilities & Campuses

MediAssist's flagship campuses each carry a clinical focus. New hires are usually assigned to one home campus but may be rotated for training.

- MediAssist Hyderabad Central (Hyderabad, Telangana) - multi-speciality flagship with emergency services.
- MediAssist Secunderabad (Secunderabad, Telangana) - cardiac sciences and critical care.
- MediAssist Bengaluru Onco Centre (Bengaluru, Karnataka) - oncology and radiation therapy.
- MediAssist Pune Speciality (Pune, Maharashtra) - orthopaedics and mother-and-child care.
- MediAssist Mysuru Clinic Hub (Mysuru, Karnataka) - day-care, dialysis and outpatient services.

Section: 5. Working Hours & Shifts

Working patterns differ between administrative and clinical staff.

- Standard office hours (administrative, billing, HR, IT): 9:00 am - 6:00 pm, Monday to Saturday, with a one-hour lunch break.
- Clinical staff work three rotating shifts: Morning 7:00 am - 3:00 pm , Afternoon 3:00 pm - 11:00 pm , and Night 11:00 pm - 7:00 am .

Section: Weekly off policy

- Administrative staff: one fixed weekly off (Sunday) plus alternate Saturdays.
- Clinical staff: one rotational weekly off scheduled by the unit in-charge, ensuring no employee works more than six consecutive days.

Section: 6. Staff ID & Access Cards

- Issuance: Your photo ID and RFID access card are issued by the Facilities desk on day one, against a signed acknowledgement.
- Lost card procedure: Report a lost card to the Facilities desk and IT Security immediately . The card is deactivated within 30 minutes; a replacement is issued within two working days against a ₹250 replacement fee.
- Access zones by role: Cards are zoned - clinical areas (ICU, OT, wards) are restricted to clinical staff and authorised personnel; server rooms to IT; pharmacy stores to pharmacy staff. Never lend your card or tailgate.

Section: 7. Dress Code

Uniforms identify roles at a glance and must be worn with a visible photo ID:

- Doctors - white coat over blue scrubs.
- Nurses - green scrubs, closed-toe footwear, hair tied back.
- Technicians - grey uniform with safety footwear where required.
- Administrative & Billing staff - formal business attire.

Section: 8. Grievance Redressal

MediAssist is committed to resolving staff grievances fairly and promptly.

- Raise a grievance with your immediate supervisor first; if unresolved or inappropriate, approach the HR Business Partner for your campus.
- Every formal grievance is acknowledged within 3 working days .
- Resolution is targeted within 21 days of acknowledgement; complex cases are communicated with an interim status.
- Grievances may be raised confidentially; retaliation is itself a disciplinary offence.

Section: 9. IT & Systems Access

- Requesting access: Raise a request on the IT Service Desk portal, approved by your HOD. Access is provisioned on the principle of least privilege.
- Acceptable use: MediAssist systems are for clinical and business use. No unauthorised software, no personal data storage on hospital devices, no circumventing security controls.
- Password policy: Minimum 12 characters with mixed case, a number and a symbol; rotate every 90 days ; never share or reuse across systems. Multi-factor authentication is mandatory for clinical and billing systems.

Section: Security

Never share your login credentials, even with a colleague or supervisor. Suspected account compromise must be reported to IT Security within 2 hours.

Section: 10. Probation & Confirmation

All new permanent employees serve a probation period during which performance, conduct and fit are assessed.

- Probation period: 6 months for clinical staff; 3 months for non-clinical staff.
- Probation may be extended once, by up to 3 months, with documented feedback.
- Confirmation is issued in writing on successful completion; benefits such as study leave accrue from the confirmation date.
- During probation the notice period for either party is 15 days.

Section: 11. Training & Professional Development

- Every clinical employee completes mandatory induction training in BLS (Basic Life Support), infection control, fire safety and POSH within 30 days of joining.
- Doctors and nurses must accrue Continuing Medical Education (CME) credits annually; study leave (5 days/yr) supports approved certification courses.
- Technicians undergo equipment-specific certification before independent operation of any biomedical device.
- An annual training calendar is published on the HR Portal; nominations are routed through HODs.

Section: 12. Emergency Codes

MediAssist  uses  a  standard  colour-code  system  across  all  campuses.  Every  employee  must  memorise  these  codes irrespective of role:

- Code Blue - adult cardiac or respiratory arrest. Dial 7777; start BLS if trained.
- Code Pink - infant or child abduction. Secure exits and alert security.
- Code Red - fire. Raise the alarm and evacuate via fire exits; never use lifts.
- Code Orange - hazardous material spill. Cordon the area; call facilities and safety.
- Code Grey - violent or aggressive person. Call security and ensure your personal safety.
- Code Yellow - internal or external disaster (mass casualty). Report to your muster point.

Section: ICU Nursing Procedures Manual

Standard Operating Procedures for Critical Care Nursing

MediAssist Health Network Nursing Education & Critical Care Document ref: NURS-ICU-008 · Version 5.0 Access: Nurses, Doctors, Admin

This manual sets out standard operating procedures (SOPs) for common ICU nursing tasks at MediAssist hospitals. Each SOP lists pre-requisites, numbered steps, monitoring parameters and documentation requirements. Procedures must be performed only by staff assessed as competent for the task.

Section: Frequency

Dressing change every 72 hours , or sooner if the dressing is soiled, loose or no longer occlusive.

Section: Equipment checklist

- Sterile gloves and sterile dressing pack
- Chlorhexidine 2% in 70% alcohol solution
- Sterile drape
- Transparent semi-permeable dressing
- Date label

Section: Procedure

- 1 Perform hand hygiene and don personal protective equipment (PPE).
- 2 Remove the old dressing and discard in the yellow (biomedical) bin.
- 3 Inspect the insertion site for redness, swelling, or discharge.
- 4 Perform hand hygiene again and apply sterile gloves.
- 5 Clean the site with chlorhexidine using a concentric (inside-out) motion.
- 6 Allow to air-dry for at least 30 seconds - do not fan or blow.
- 7 Apply the new transparent dressing.
- 8 Label the dressing with the date and your initials.
- 9 Document the procedure and site assessment in the care record.

Section: Escalate

Notify the doctor immediately for: purulent discharge, fever > 38.5 °C with no other source, or tracking erythema extending > 2 cm from the insertion site.

Section: Initial settings

| Parameter        | Starting Value                          |
|------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| Mode             | Assist-control / Volume-control (AC/VC) |
| Tidal volume     | 6-8 mL/kg ideal body weight             |
| Respiratory rate | 12-16 breaths/min                       |
| FiO₂             | Start 1.0, then titrate to SpO₂ 94-98%  |
| PEEP             | 5 cmH₂O (default)                       |

Section: Hourly monitoring

- SpO₂, end-tidal CO₂ (EtCO₂)
- Peak and plateau airway pressures
- Delivered tidal volume
- Ventilator alarm status

Section: Alarm response

| Alarm                      | Immediate Action                                                                            |
|----------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| High pressure (> 40 cmH₂O) | Check for secretions / kinking / coughing →suction if indicated →call doctor if it persists |
| Low SpO₂ (< 90%)           | Increase FiO₂ →assess airway and circuit →call doctor immediately                           |

Section: Ventilator bundle (VAP prevention)

- Head of bed (HOB) elevation 30-45°
- Oral care with chlorhexidine every 4 hours
- Subglottic suctioning where available
- Daily sedation vacation and readiness-to-wean assessment

Section: Sizing

Adult: 14-16 Fr . Paediatric: select by age-appropriate formula.

Section: Insertion procedure

- 1 Measure from the tip of the nose to the earlobe to the xiphoid process; mark the tube.
- 2 Lubricate the tip with water-soluble gel.
- 3 Insert through the nostril, advancing gently.
- 4 Ask the patient to swallow (sips of water if permitted) as you advance.
- 5 Advance to the measured length.
- 6 Confirm position by aspirate pH < 5.5 AND chest X-ray before first feed.

Section: Feeding & gastric residual monitoring

| Situation         | Action                                 |
|-------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Initiation        | Start at 20 mL/hr                      |
| If GRV < 200 mL   | Increase by 20 mL/hr every 4 hours     |
| If GRV 200-400 mL | Hold feed 1 hour and recheck           |
| If GRV > 400 mL   | Hold feed; notify dietitian and doctor |

<!-- image -->

Section: Site selection (order of preference)

Forearm → antecubital fossa → dorsum of hand. Avoid the legs except in emergency.

Section: Cannula sizing by indication

| Indication                | Recommended Gauge   |
|---------------------------|---------------------|
| Blood transfusion         | ≥ 18G               |
| Rapid fluid resuscitation | ≥ 16G               |
| Routine medication        | 20-22G              |
| Paediatric < 5 kg         | 24G                 |
| Paediatric 5-20 kg        | 22G                 |
| Paediatric > 20 kg        | 20G                 |

Section: Failed attempt protocol

- Maximum 2 attempts per nurse .
- After 2 failed attempts, escalate to a senior nurse or doctor.

Section: Cannula change

Replace every 72-96 hours , or immediately if there are signs of phlebitis (Visual Infusion Phlebitis [VIP] score ≥ 2).

Section: Principle

Restraint is a last resort to prevent imminent harm (self-extubation, falls) and must never be used as routine care or for staff convenience.

Section: Authorisation

- Requires a verbal doctor's order, with a written order within 1 hour .
- Re-authorisation is required every 24 hours .

Section: Monitoring while restrained

- Neurovascular check every 2 hours - circulation, sensation and movement in the restrained limb.
- Offer range-of-motion exercises every 2 hours.
- Document every check.

Section: Documentation requirements

- Reason for restraint and alternatives tried
- Time applied and type of restraint
- All monitoring observations
- Communication with the family

Section: SOP 6 - Endotracheal Suctioning

Suctioning clears secretions to maintain airway patency in the intubated patient. It is performed only when clinically indicated, not on a fixed routine.

Section: Indications

- Visible or audible secretions / coarse breath sounds
- Rising peak airway pressure or falling tidal volume
- Desaturation or a sawtooth pattern on the flow-volume loop

Section: Procedure

- 1 Hand hygiene; don gloves, apron and eye protection.
- 2 Pre-oxygenate with FiO₂ 1.0 for 30-60 seconds.
- 3 Use a closed in-line suction catheter where available; select catheter ≤ half the ETT internal diameter.
- 4 Insert without suction; apply suction on withdrawal for no more than 10-15 seconds.
- 5 Limit to two passes; allow recovery between passes.
- 6 Return ventilator settings to baseline and reassess.
- 7 Document secretion colour, volume and the patient's tolerance.

Section: Safety

Stop suctioning immediately for SpO₂ < 90%, bradycardia, or a new arrhythmia, and re-oxygenate the patient.

Section: Risk assessment

Assess  every  ICU  patient  using  the Braden Scale on  admission  and  at  least  every  24  hours.  A  score  ≤  18  indicates increased risk.

Section: Preventive bundle

- Repositioning - turn the patient every 2 hours and document the position.
- Support surface - use a pressure-redistributing mattress for high-risk patients.
- Skin inspection - inspect bony prominences each shift.
- Moisture management - keep the skin clean and dry; apply barrier cream as needed.
- Nutrition - refer at-risk patients to the dietitian.

Section: Staging quick reference

- Stage 1 - non-blanching erythema with intact skin.
- Stage 2 - partial-thickness skin loss; a shallow open ulcer.
- Stage 3 - full-thickness skin loss with fat visible.
- Stage 4 - full-thickness tissue loss with muscle or bone visible.

Section: Infection Control Guidelines

Standard Precautions and Outbreak Management

MediAssist Health Network Hospital Infection Control Committee Document ref: NURS-IC-009 · Version 4.2 Access: Nurses, Doctors, Admin

These  guidelines  define  the  standard  precautions  and  outbreak-management  procedures  that  apply  across  all MediAssist facilities. Adherence is mandatory for all clinical staff and is monitored by the Infection Control team.

Section: 1. Five Moments of Hand Hygiene

The WHO 'Five Moments' framework is the foundation of infection prevention. Perform hand hygiene at each of these moments:

- 1 Before patient contact - to protect the patient from your hands.
- 2 Before an aseptic procedure - to protect the patient from infection.
- 3 After body-fluid exposure risk - to protect yourself and the environment.
- 4 After patient contact - to protect yourself and the environment.
- 5 After contact with patient surroundings - to protect yourself and the environment.

Section: Technique

Duration: alcohol-based hand rub 20-30 seconds ; soap-and-water handwash 40-60 seconds . Wash with soap and water when hands are visibly soiled or after caring for a patient with C. difficile.

Section: 2. PPE Selection Guide

| Scenario                        | Gloves   | Apron/Gown   | Mask     | Eye Protection   |
|---------------------------------|----------|--------------|----------|------------------|
| Routine patient contact         | Yes      | No           | No       | No               |
| Contact with blood/body fluids  | Yes      | Yes          | Surgical | If splash risk   |
| Suspected respiratory infection | Yes      | Yes          | N95      | Goggles/shield   |
| Aerosol-generating procedure    | Yes      | Full gown    | N95/PAPR | Full face shield |
| Isolation room (contact)        | Yes      | Yes          | Surgical | As indicated     |

Aerosol-generating  procedures  include  intubation,  extubation,  bronchoscopy,  open  suctioning  and  non-invasive ventilation.

Section: 3. Standard Precautions

Standard precautions apply to all patients regardless of diagnosis . They treat blood and all body fluids, secretions and excretions  (except  sweat),  non-intact  skin,  and  mucous  membranes  as  potentially  infectious.  They  include  hand hygiene, appropriate PPE, safe injection practice, and safe handling of sharps and linen.

Section: 4. Transmission-Based Precautions

| Type     | Example Organisms                   | Key Measures                                                       |
|----------|-------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Contact  | MRSA, VRE, C. difficile, norovirus  | Single room preferred; dedicated equipment; visitors gown + gloves |
| Droplet  | Influenza, pertussis, meningococcus | Surgical mask within 1 metre; single room preferred                |
| Airborne | TB, measles, chickenpox             | Negative-pressure room; N95; limit patient transport               |

Section: 5. Healthcare-Associated Infection (HAI) Surveillance

The four HAIs under active surveillance are:

- CAUTI - catheter-associated urinary tract infection.
- CLABSI - central line-associated bloodstream infection.
- VAP - ventilator-associated pneumonia.
- SSI - surgical site infection.
- Any suspected HAI must be reported to the infection control nurse within 24 hours .
- A root-cause analysis (RCA) is conducted for every confirmed HAI, with learning shared at the monthly infection control committee.

Section: 6. Outbreak Management Protocol

An outbreak is defined as two or more linked cases within 72 hours .

Section: Notification chain

Ward in-charge → Infection Control team → Chief Medical Officer (CMO).

Section: Control measures

- Cohort affected patients; dedicate staff and equipment where possible.
- Implement visitor restrictions.
- Enhance environmental cleaning frequency and audit.
- Issue a staff-briefing communication using the standard template.

Section: 7. Waste Segregation

| Bin Colour                 | Waste Type                         | Examples                                   |
|----------------------------|------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Yellow                     | Biomedical / infectious            | Dressings, blood-soaked items, body fluids |
| Red                        | Anatomical / contaminated plastics | Tubing, catheters, IV sets                 |
| Blue / White (translucent) | Glassware & sharps                 | Vials, ampoules, needles (puncture-proof)  |
| Black                      | General (non-hazardous)            | Office and domestic waste                  |
| White (cytotoxic)          | Cytotoxic / pharmaceutical         | Chemotherapy waste                         |

Section: Practice

Segregate at the point of generation. Biomedical waste bins are removed at least once per shift, and more frequently when ¾ full. Document collection in the waste register.

Section: 8. Needlestick & Sharps Injury Management

A sharps injury is a medical emergency for the exposed worker and must be acted on immediately.

- 1 Encourage the wound to bleed gently; wash with soap and running water (do not scrub).
- 2 Do not suck the wound; for splashes to eyes/mouth, irrigate with water or saline.
- 3 Report to the shift in-charge and attend the Emergency department / occupational health without delay .
- 4 Identify the source patient; risk-assess for HIV, Hepatitis B and Hepatitis C.
- 5 Commence post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) if indicated - ideally within 2 hours, and no later than 72 hours for HIV exposure.
- 6 Complete the incident report and arrange baseline and follow-up serology.

Section: Prevention

Never recap needles. Dispose of sharps at the point of use in the puncture-proof sharps container, filled no more than three-quarters.

Section: 9. Antimicrobial Stewardship (nursing role)

- Administer antimicrobials on time; delays compromise efficacy and resistance control.
- Prompt review of IV-to-oral switch when the patient is improving and afebrile.
- Ensure cultures are sent before the first antibiotic dose where feasible.
- Flag to the medical team any antibiotic continued beyond review date or without a documented indication.

Section: 10. Isolation Signage

Colour-coded door signs tell staff and visitors which precautions are in force before they enter:

- Contact (yellow sign) - contact precautions in force; gown and gloves before entry.
- Droplet (blue sign) - droplet precautions in force; surgical mask within 1 metre.
- Airborne (red sign) - airborne precautions in force; N95 plus a negative-pressure room.
